content:
# Leviticus Chapter 14 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 66r–66v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_014_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_014_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses: **2** "This is the instruction for the person with tzara'at on the day they are declared clean. They shall be brought to the priest, **3** and the priest shall go outside the camp to examine them. If the priest sees that the tzara'at infection has been healed in the one who had tzara'at, **4** the priest shall order two living clean birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop to be brought for the one to be purified.

**5** "The priest shall order one of the birds to be slaughtered over fresh water in a clay pot. **6** He shall take the living bird along with the cedar wood, the scarlet yarn, and the hyssop, and dip them together with the living bird in the blood of the bird that was slaughtered over the fresh water. **7** He shall sprinkle it seven times on the one to be purified from the tzara'at, and declare them clean. Then he shall release the living bird into the open field.

**8** "The one being purified shall wash their clothes, shave off all their hair, and bathe in water — and they will be clean. After this they may re-enter the camp, but they must live outside their tent for seven days. **9** On the seventh day they shall shave off all their hair: their head, their beard, their eyebrows — every bit of hair. They shall wash their clothes and bathe their body in water, and they will be clean.

**10** "On the eighth day they shall bring two male lambs without blemish and one ewe lamb in its first year without blemish, together with three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil as a grain offering, and one log of oil. **11** The priest who performs the purification ritual shall present the person being purified, along with these offerings, before the LORD at the entrance to the tent of meeting.

**12** "The priest shall take one of the male lambs and offer it as a guilt offering, along with the log of oil, and wave them as a wave offering before the LORD. **13** He shall slaughter the lamb in the place where the sin offering and the burnt offering are slaughtered — at the holy site — for the guilt offering, like the sin offering, belongs to the priest; it is most holy. **14** The priest shall take some of the blood of the guilt offering and apply it to the lobe of the right ear of the one being purified, to the thumb of their right hand, and to the big toe of their right foot.

**15** "The priest shall then take some of the log of oil and pour it into the palm of his own left hand, **16** and with his right finger dip it from the oil in his left palm and sprinkle some of the oil with his finger seven times before the LORD. **17** The rest of the oil in his palm the priest shall apply to the lobe of the right ear of the one being purified, to the thumb of their right hand, and to the big toe of their right foot — on top of the blood of the guilt offering. **18** Whatever remains of the oil in the priest's palm he shall pour on the head of the one being purified. So the priest shall make atonement for them before the LORD.

**19** "The priest shall then offer the sin offering and make atonement for the one being purified from their uncleanness. After that, he shall slaughter the burnt offering. **20** The priest shall offer the burnt offering and the grain offering on the altar. So the priest shall make atonement for them, and they will be clean.

**21** "If, however, the person is poor and cannot afford all this, they shall bring one male lamb as a guilt offering, to be waved in order to make atonement for them, together with one-tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil as a grain offering, and a log of oil; **22** and two turtledoves or two young pigeons — whatever they can afford — one to serve as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering.

**23** "On the eighth day they shall bring them to the priest at the entrance to the tent of meeting, before the LORD, for their purification. **24** The priest shall take the lamb of the guilt offering and the log of oil, and wave them as a wave offering before the LORD. **25** He shall slaughter the lamb of the guilt offering, and the priest shall take some of its blood and apply it to the lobe of the right ear of the one being purified, to the thumb of their right hand, and to the big toe of their right foot.

**26** "The priest shall pour some of the oil into his left palm, **27** and with his right finger sprinkle some of the oil from his left palm seven times before the LORD. **28** The priest shall apply some of the oil in his palm to the lobe of the right ear of the one being purified, to the thumb of their right hand, and to the big toe of their right foot — in the same places where the blood of the guilt offering was applied. **29** The rest of the oil in the priest's palm he shall pour on the head of the one being purified, to make atonement for them before the LORD.

**30** "Then the priest shall offer the turtledoves or young pigeons — whichever the person could afford — **31** one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering, along with the grain offering. So the priest shall make atonement before the LORD for the one being purified. **32** This is the instruction for anyone who has tzara'at and cannot afford the standard offerings for their purification."

**33** The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron: **34** "When you enter the land of Canaan, which I am giving you as your possession, and I place a tzara'at-like affliction in a house in the land you are about to possess, **35** the owner of the house shall come and tell the priest, 'There appears to me to be something like a disease in my house.' **36** The priest shall order the house to be emptied before he goes in to examine the disease — so that nothing in the house will be declared unclean — and after that the priest shall go in to examine the house.

**37** "The priest shall examine the disease. If the disease is in the walls of the house, with greenish or reddish depressions that appear to go deeper than the surface of the wall, **38** the priest shall go out of the house door and shut the house up for seven days. **39** On the seventh day the priest shall come back and examine it. If the disease has spread in the walls of the house, **40** the priest shall order the stones in which the disease is to be removed and thrown into an unclean place outside the city. **41** He shall have the inside of the house completely scraped, and the plaster that is scraped off shall be dumped in an unclean place outside the city. **42** Then new stones shall be taken and placed where the old stones were, and fresh plaster shall be applied to the house.

**43** "If the disease breaks out again in the house after the stones have been removed and the house has been scraped and re-plastered, **44** the priest shall come and examine it. If the disease has spread in the house, it is a destructive tzara'at in the house; it is unclean. **45** The house must be torn down — its stones, timber, and all the plaster — and carried outside the city to an unclean place.

**46** "Anyone who enters the house while it is shut up will be unclean until evening. **47** Anyone who lies down in the house must wash their clothes. Anyone who eats in the house must wash their clothes.

**48** "But if the priest comes to examine it and finds that the disease has not spread in the house after the house was re-plastered, he shall declare the house clean, for the disease has been healed. **49** To purify the house he shall take two birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop. **50** He shall slaughter one of the birds over fresh water in a clay pot. **51** Then he shall take the cedar wood, the hyssop, the scarlet yarn, and the living bird, dip them in the blood of the slaughtered bird and in the fresh water, and sprinkle the house seven times. **52** He shall purify the house with the blood of the bird, the fresh water, the living bird, the cedar wood, the hyssop, and the scarlet yarn. **53** Then he shall release the living bird outside the city into the open field. So he shall make atonement for the house, and it will be clean.

**54** This is the instruction for every case of tzara'at — for scaly skin diseases, **55** for tzara'at in garments and houses, **56** for swellings and scabs and shiny spots — **57** to determine when something is unclean and when it is clean. This is the instruction for tzara'at.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The Drama of the Two Birds (vv.1–7)

The two-bird ritual is one of the most arresting ceremonies in the entire Torah, and it deserves to be seen clearly. Two living birds. One is killed over fresh spring water — the word *mayim chayyim* literally means "living water," water still connected to its source. The blood disperses into that living water. Then the second bird, still alive, is dipped in that blood-water mixture along with three symbolic materials — cedar, scarlet thread, and hyssop — and released into the open sky.

The ritual speaks in two movements that together accomplish what words alone cannot: *death absorbed, life restored, departure effected.* The killed bird represents the condition that had cut the person off from community and from God's dwelling. The released bird carries that condition away — outside the camp, into the wild, beyond reach. This is not merely symbolic theater. The ancient Israelite understood that something real was being transacted. The structural parallel to the Yom Kippur ceremony (Leviticus 16), where one goat is slaughtered and another is sent into the wilderness carrying the sins of Israel, is unmistakable. In both rituals, the two-part animal action dramatizes a two-part theological reality: the removal of guilt and the restoration of life.

The phrase "declare them clean" — the priest's verbal announcement — is performative speech. It does not merely describe a reality; it enacts one. The priest functions as a mediator whose declaration brings the person back across the threshold from excluded to included, from outside to inside, from the realm of death back to the realm of life.

### The Purification Triad: Cedar, Scarlet, Hyssop (vv.4, 6, 49, 51–52)

These three materials recur across the Hebrew Bible's purification repertoire (see also Numbers 19:6, in the red heifer ritual). Cedar wood — tall, aromatic, enduring — represents vitality and incorruptibility. Hyssop — small, low-growing, bushy — is consistently associated with sprinkling rituals and with the capacity to cleanse (Psalm 51:7: "Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean"). Scarlet yarn, made from crimson dye, may carry associations with blood and with life-force. Together the triad forms a sensory package: sight (the red), smell (the cedar), texture (the hyssop's bushy form ideal for sprinkling). The ritual engages the whole person, not merely the intellect.

### The Eighth Day: Threshold Theology (vv.10–20)

The structure of time in this chapter is theologically deliberate. The first-phase ritual (two birds, vv.2–7) takes place outside the camp — the unclean space where the person had been exiled. After that, they re-enter the camp but cannot yet enter their own tent: they are in a liminal state, neither fully outside nor fully inside. Seven days pass — the number of completion, the week of creation — and on the seventh day they shave and bathe again. Then comes the eighth day.

The eighth day is the day beyond the week. It is the day of new beginnings, the day that exists on the far side of completion. Circumcision happens on the eighth day (Leviticus 12:3). The priests were consecrated over seven days and began their ministry on the eighth (Leviticus 9:1). The eighth day is where creation hands off to covenant. The person healed of tzara'at, by arriving at the eighth day with their offerings, is being re-incorporated not just into human society but into the covenant community.

### Blood and Oil on the Right Side (vv.14–18, 25–29)

The application of blood and oil to the right ear lobe, right thumb, and right big toe is not arbitrary. The same three points appear in the consecration of Aaron and his sons as priests (Leviticus 8:23–24). The healed person is, in microcosm, undergoing a form of re-consecration — a restoration of full human dignity and covenant standing. The ear is consecrated to hear the word of God; the hand to do the work of God; the foot to walk in the ways of God. The blood of the guilt offering grounds the rite in the logic of atonement; the oil layered on top of the blood speaks of blessing, of the Spirit's presence, of abundance poured over the site of former wound.

### The Poverty Provision (vv.21–32)

One of the great moral impulses of Leviticus is its insistence that the doors of purification must not be economically barred. The standard ritual requires two lambs, a ewe, fine flour, and oil — a significant financial burden for a subsistence-farming household. The poverty provision cuts this substantially: one lamb, and two birds instead of two more lambs. The theological logic is identical; the cost is calibrated to what can actually be given. This same principle governs the birth offering in Leviticus 12 (pigeons or turtledoves for those who cannot afford a lamb). God does not demand of the poor what only the wealthy can give. The structure of the sacrificial system, read carefully, contains within itself a theology of grace: what is required is proportionate to what has been given.

### House-Tzara'at: Extending the Framework to the Land (vv.33–53)

The extension of tzara'at diagnosis to houses — a discoloration of walls, a spreading damp or mold — is more than a practical matter of property hygiene. The legislation is explicitly framed as future: "When you enter the land of Canaan, which I am giving you as your possession." House-tzara'at is a Canaan phenomenon. The land itself can become afflicted; the dwelling can carry impurity as the body can.

This carries a profound theological implication: the purity of the covenant community extends outward from person to garment (Leviticus 13:47–59) to house to land. Israel's holiness is not a purely interior, spiritual matter — it has spatial, material, and communal dimensions. If the land itself can carry defilement, then Israel's faithfulness or unfaithfulness has consequences for the territory. This anticipates the land "vomiting out" its inhabitants in Leviticus 18:28 — the land is a moral and spiritual environment, not merely a geographical one.

The priest's procedure for the house is structurally parallel to the person: examination, isolation, re-examination, possible removal of infected material (stones), possible demolition, or — if the disease is healed — the same two-bird purification rite that restores a person. The house, like the person, is either declared clean or declared to be beyond reclamation.

### The Summary (vv.54–57)

The chapter closes with a brief legal formula cataloguing what has just been covered: skin disease of persons, garments, houses. The formula "to determine when something is unclean and when it is clean" is the practical aim. But underneath the legal language is a pastoral theology: the purpose of all this instruction is to give both priest and people a reliable way to navigate the boundary between life and death, inclusion and exclusion, the holy and the unclean — and above all, to provide a clear and accessible path back from the far side of that boundary.

Tzara'at, in the theological imagination of Leviticus, is death in miniature — it is flesh decaying while still alive (see Numbers 12:12, where Moses begs that Miriam not be "like one dead, whose flesh is half consumed when he comes out of his mother's womb"). The elaborate restoration rituals of chapter 14 are, correspondingly, resurrection in miniature: life restored to what had been as good as dead, re-entrance into the community of the living, re-consecration to the God who dwells among them.

### LXX Observations

The Septuagint (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 99–100) renders *tzara'at* consistently as *lepra*, a Greek medical term for scaly skin conditions. While this eventually generated the mistaken identification with what we call leprosy (Hansen's disease), the Greek translators understood *lepra* broadly, as the Hebrew text itself suggests — applying the same diagnostic category to mold on walls and stains on garments. The LXX's *zōn* (living) for *chayyim* in "living water" (v.5) preserves the force of the Hebrew: this is not standing water but water with life still in it, water connected to its source, water that can carry life across the boundary of death.

The phrase *aphesis* — "release, sending away" — used in the LXX for the release of the living bird (v.7) is cognate with the New Testament word for forgiveness (*aphesis hamartiōn*, "the release of sins"). Whether or not the LXX translators intended a theological resonance with the Yom Kippur *scapegoat* (*apopompaios*), the shared vocabulary invites the reader to feel the connection: in all these rituals, something is being *released* — sent away, sent out, no longer held against the one who was bound by it.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 66r–66v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 99–100 (LXX).*
